docs.title: BlinkUI
docs.text: Build mobile apps in pure Python

docs.title: What is BlinkUI?
docs.text:
BlinkUI is a mobile app framework that lets Python developers build iOS and Android apps without learning Swift, Kotlin, or JavaScript.

Write your app in pure Python. BlinkUI's C runtime handles rendering, animations, and events natively on device. 

from blinkui import Screen, state
from blinkui.components import VStack, Text, Button, Heading

class HomeScreen(Screen):
    count = state(0)

    def build(self):
        return VStack(
            Heading("Hello from BlinkUI"),
            Text(f"Count: {self.count}").size(48).bold(),
            Button("Tap Me").on_tap(self.increment),
        ).spacing(16).padding(24)

    def increment(self):
        self.count += 1

docs.title: Why BlinkUI?
docs.text: BlinkUI React Native Flutter Language Python JavaScript Dart Python ecosystem ✅ Full ❌ ❌ AI/ML integration ✅ Via API ❌ ❌ Native UI ✅ ✅ ❌ Learning curve Low Medium High

docs.title: Install
docs.text: pip install blinkui

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
pip install blinkui
blink new myapp
cd myapp
blink run

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
BlinkUI is built in two layers:

C Runtime — inspired by Facebook's Hermes engine:

  • Node tree — every UI element in memory
  • Reconciler — diffs trees, patches only what changed
  • Event system — native interactions call Python callbacks
  • Animation engine — 60fps running entirely in C
  • CPython host — Python embedded inside iOS and Android

Python Framework — what developers write in:

  • state() — reactive variables, UI updates automatically
  • Screen — base class for every screen
  • Router — navigation with data passing
  • Theme — iOS-inspired design system
  • HTTP client — async requests, never freezes UI
  • Async storage — persistent key-value store

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Getting Started — build your first app
  • State Management — reactive state
  • Navigation — move between screens
  • Components — full component reference

docs.title: Install BlinkUI
docs.text: pip install blinkui

docs.title: Create your first app
docs.text:
blink new myapp
cd myapp
blink run

Your app is running. Open screens/home.py to start editing.

docs.title: Project structure
docs.text:
myapp/
├── main.py              # entry point
├── blink.toml           # project config
├── screens/
│   ├── home.py          # home screen
│   └── detail.py        # detail screen
├── components/          # your custom components
└── assets/
    └── images/

docs.title: Your first screen
docs.text:
Every screen inherits from Screen and implements build(): 

from blinkui import Screen, state
from blinkui.components import VStack, Text, Button, Heading

class HomeScreen(Screen):
    count = state(0)

    def build(self):
        return VStack(
            Heading("My First App"),
            Text(f"Count: {self.count}").size(32).bold(),
            Button("Tap Me").on_tap(self.increment),
        ).spacing(16).padding(24)

    def increment(self):
        self.count += 1

    def on_mount(self):
        print("Screen mounted")

docs.title: Entry point
docs.text:
main.py sets up routing and launches the app: 

from blinkui import Router
from screens.home import HomeScreen
from screens.detail import DetailScreen

Router(
    routes={
        "home":   HomeScreen,
        "detail": DetailScreen,
    },
    entry="home"
)

docs.title: CLI commands
docs.text:
blink new myapp          # create a new app
blink run                # run on simulator
blink run --hot          # hot reload on save
blink run --device       # run on connected device
blink install pandas     # install Python packages
blink build ios          # build for App Store
blink build android      # build for Play Store

docs.title: Installing packages
docs.text:
blink install requests
blink install pandas
blink install numpy

Packages install into .blink/packages/ and are bundled with your app at build time. 

# use them normally in your screens
import requests
import pandas as pd

docs.title: HTTP Client
docs.text: BlinkUI includes a built in async HTTP client. All requests run in the background so the UI never freezes.

docs.title: Basic usage
docs.text:
from blinkui.http import get, post

class HomeScreen(Screen):
    data  = state(None)
    error = state(None)

    async def on_mount(self):
        response = await get("https://api.example.com/users")
        if response.ok:
            self.data = response.data
        else:
            self.error = response.error

docs.title: POST request
docs.text:
async def create_user(self):
    response = await post("https://api.example.com/users", {
        "name":  "John Doe",
        "email": "john@example.com"
    })
    if response.ok:
        self.navigate("success")

docs.title: Set base URL
docs.text:
# in main.py — set once, use everywhere
from blinkui.http import set_base_url
set_base_url("https://api.myapp.com")

# now use relative paths anywhere
response = await get("/users")
response = await post("/users", data)

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
from blinkui.http import set_token

# sets Authorization: Bearer <token> on all requests
set_token("my_jwt_token")

docs.title: All methods
docs.text:
from blinkui.http import get, post, put, patch, delete

response = await get("/users")
response = await post("/users", {"name": "John"})
response = await put("/users/42", {"name": "Updated"})
response = await patch("/users/42", {"email": "new@email.com"})
response = await delete("/users/42")

docs.title: Response object
docs.text:
response.ok       # True if status 200-299
response.status   # HTTP status code
response.data     # parsed JSON as dict or list
response.text     # raw response string
response.error    # error message if failed
response.headers  # response headers dict

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text: BlinkUI uses a stack based router. Screens push onto the stack and pop off when going back.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
from blinkui import Router
from screens.home import HomeScreen
from screens.profile import ProfileScreen
from screens.settings import SettingsScreen

Router(
    routes={
        "home":     HomeScreen,
        "profile":  ProfileScreen,
        "settings": SettingsScreen,
    },
    entry="home"
)

docs.title: Navigate forward
docs.text:
def go_profile(self):
    self.navigate("profile")

docs.title: Pass data between screens
docs.text:
def go_profile(self):
    self.navigate("profile", data={
        "user_id": 42,
        "name": "John"
    })

Receive it in the next screen: 

class ProfileScreen(Screen):
    def build(self):
        user_id = self.route_data.get("user_id")
        name    = self.route_data.get("name")
        return VStack(
            Text(f"Profile: {name}"),
        )

docs.title: Go back
docs.text:
def go_back(self):
    self._navigator.pop()

docs.title: Replace current screen
docs.text:
# replaces current screen without adding to stack
def go_login(self):
    self._navigator.replace("login")

docs.title: Reset stack
docs.text:
# clear entire stack and start fresh
def logout(self):
    self._navigator.reset("login")

docs.title: Lifecycle hooks
docs.text:
class HomeScreen(Screen):
    def on_mount(self):
        # called when screen appears
        print("mounted")

    def on_unmount(self):
        # called when screen is removed
        print("unmounted")

docs.title: State Management
docs.text: BlinkUI uses reactive state. When a state() variable changes the screen re-renders automatically.

docs.title: Declaring state
docs.text:
from blinkui import Screen, state

class CounterScreen(Screen):
    count    = state(0)
    name     = state("John")
    loading  = state(False)
    items    = state([])

docs.title: Using state in build
docs.text:
def build(self):
    return VStack(
        Text(f"Hello {self.name}"),
        Text(f"Count: {self.count}"),
    )

docs.title: Updating state
docs.text:
Assign a new value anywhere in your screen: 

def increment(self):
    self.count += 1

def set_name(self, name):
    self.name = name

def load_data(self):
    self.loading = True
    # fetch data...
    self.items = ["item1", "item2"]
    self.loading = False

Every assignment triggers a re-render automatically.

docs.title: Conditional rendering
docs.text:
def build(self):
    return VStack(
        Spinner() if self.loading else Text("Ready"),
        Button("Load").on_tap(self.load),
    )

docs.title: State with async
docs.text:
async def on_mount(self):
    self.loading = True
    response = await get("https://api.example.com/data")
    self.items = response.data
    self.loading = False

docs.title: Storage
docs.text: BlinkUI includes persistent async storage. Data survives app restarts.

docs.title: Basic usage
docs.text:
from blinkui.storage import storage

# save
await storage.set("token", "abc123")

# get
token = await storage.get("token")

# get with fallback
theme = await storage.get("theme", default="light")

# delete
await storage.delete("token")

# check exists
if await storage.has("token"):
    self.navigate("dashboard")
else:
    self.navigate("login")

docs.title: Storing complex data
docs.text:
# dicts and lists are serialized automatically
await storage.set("user", {
    "id":    42,
    "name":  "John",
    "email": "john@example.com"
})

user = await storage.get("user")
print(user["name"])  # John

docs.title: Batch operations
docs.text:
# save multiple at once
await storage.set_many({
    "token":    "abc123",
    "user_id":  42,
    "theme":    "dark"
})

# get multiple at once
result = await storage.get_many(["token", "user_id", "theme"])

docs.title: All keys and clear
docs.text:
# get all keys
keys = await storage.keys()

# get everything
everything = await storage.get_all()

# clear all data — useful for logout
await storage.clear()

docs.title: Common pattern — auth check on mount
docs.text:
class SplashScreen(Screen):
    async def on_mount(self):
        token = await storage.get("token")
        if token:
            self.navigate("home")
        else:
            self.navigate("login")
